nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; found in deeper rocky reef areas with rich growths of sponges and algae, generally at depths of 15–40 meters

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on filamentous algae, diatoms, sponges, and tunicates in the wild. In captivity, tends to accept Spirulina, marine algae preparations, high-quality angelfish formula, mysid shrimp, and frozen meaty foods. Generally advised to offer feedings at least three times per day to maintain condition.

summary: The Bluespotted Angelfish is a Philippine endemic generally considered suitable only for experienced aquarists. It tends to be semi-aggressive, particularly toward other angelfish, and is widely regarded as not reef-safe due to its tendency to nip at corals and clam mantles.
careNotes:
The Bluespotted Angelfish (Chaetodontoplus caeruleopunctatus) is endemic to the Philippine Archipelago, where it has been documented on rocky reef slopes at depths of 15 to 40 meters among rich growths of sponges and algae. It is generally considered suitable only for advanced or experienced aquarists, owing to the complexity of meeting its dietary needs and the risk it poses to reef invertebrates.

A tank of at least 500 liters (roughly 130 US gallons) is widely recommended, with a well-established rockwork structure that provides caves and overhangs. The species uses the full water column and benefits from both open areas for cruising and sheltered retreats.

The Bluespotted Angelfish is widely regarded as not reef-safe. It has been known to nip at the mantles of tridacnid clams, as well as at stony and soft corals, making it a poor fit for mixed reef systems. Keeping it alongside other angelfish or conspecifics is generally advised against; most aquarists house it singly with tangs, clownfish, gobies, blennies, and other non-angelfish community fish.

In the wild, this species feeds on sponges, tunicates, filamentous algae, and diatoms. In captivity, varied offerings presented multiple times per day are generally advised to maintain condition. Transitioning a newly acquired specimen to prepared foods may require patience.

The Bluespotted Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning individuals are documented to begin life as females, with dominant fish later transitioning to male. Captive breeding has not been established.
breedingNotes: Documented as a protogynous hermaphrodite; individuals are reported to start as females and dominant individuals transition to male. Spawning produces pelagic eggs and larvae, but successful captive breeding has not been established in the published literature.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (cites 5.5 in / 14 cm, lower than other sources), tank size (75 gal vs 125 gal consensus), reef safety (no vs with-caution). Care level listed as intermediate vs advanced elsewhere.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, max TL 20.0 cm, Philippine endemic, reef-associated habitat at 15–40 m depth, family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cites adult size 12 cm, tank minimum 1000 L (outlier vs 473 L consensus), temperature 22–28°C. Used for temperature cross-check and size averaging.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 8 in / 20 cm, tank 125 gal, temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.021–1.025, care level difficult/advanced, semi-aggressive temperament, reef not safe. Also confirmed protogynous hermaphroditism and lifespan 10–15 years.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite. Pelagic eggs and larvae documented in the wild; no established captive breeding protocol reported in the literature.
